items.prompt: Which four strategies does Chapter 11 develop beyond Chapter 4 fundamentals?
choices: Active learning, weak supervision, self-supervised learning, and hybrid crowd–expert pipelines
Only crowdsourcing platforms and inter-annotator agreement
Only synthetic data generation and GAN training
Only storage versioning and lineage tracking
items.explain: Section 11.1 frames the chapter around those four complementary strategies.

items.prompt: Active learning and weak supervision are mutually exclusive; teams should pick only one.
items.explain: Table 11.1 and the surrounding prose state the strategies are complementary and often combined.

items.prompt: According to Table 11.1, what does active learning primarily optimize?
choices: Which unlabeled items humans should label next
Representations learned before any task labels
Scale of labels from distant databases only
items.explain: Active learning selects the queried subset for human labeling when labels are expensive.

items.prompt: Name one primary risk of weak supervision listed in the chapter comparison.
items.rubric: Mention noisy/conflicting sources or unvalidated weak labels entering training.
items.sample_answer: Noisy or conflicting sources, or unvalidated weak labels entering training.

items.prompt: Self-supervised pretraining removes all need for labeled data on the final task.
items.explain: Table 11.1 notes that SSL still needs labeled data for fine-tuning the final task.
